text: along the road to the Market. He knew she followed by the sound of her footfalls on the gravel, the squishing when she put weight on her damaged boot, the little noises she made when her feet slipped or she stepped in a particularly deep puddle . Despite that evidence, part of him wanted to look back, just to make sure she was there . Lara’s agreement to his offer was logical; it provided her security and comfort she’d probably never known. With her added stipulation that he assist in the search for her missing sister, she stood only to gain from the arrangement. It was a strange turn — humans, in his experience, were rarely rational in their decisions . Their roles, clearly, were reversed in this case. There had been no logic behind his offer. Only curiosity, an inexplicable need to know what set her apart from everyone else . “So, where are we going?” Lara asked . “You already know.” Ronin turned his head to the right, where the lights of the Bot District shone in contrast to the dark sky . “I know we’re going there . But where in there is your… residence ? Who the hell uses words like that, anyway ?” “I do,” he replied, glancing at her over his shoulder, “and my residence is in the northwest corner of the district. Not far from the Market .” There was something refreshing about conversing with her. Though no two runs in the Dust were ever the same, they were predictable in their own ways — the same prevalent dangers, the same volatile weather, the same reminders of a lost world. And, always, the sense he was on the verge of discerning his true programming . But he couldn’t predict what Lara would say or do from moment to moment. He could learn the signs of a coming outburst, perhaps, but it was impossible to know what would come out of her mouth. Back in the shack, she’d been a few hundredths of a second away from slapping him across the face. It had sent a strange, not unpleasant pulse through him . “So, uh…we gonna run into any, uh…any gearheads ?” The trepidation in her tone and the soft uncertainty of her voice made Ronin frown . “Gearheads?” He searched his memory for the term. If he knew it, it was lost in the Black Out, like so much else. Was it just another slur for his kind? “Of course there will be bots .” “Not just bots . Them .” “You’ll have to be more specific, Lara Brooks .” She muttered, too low for him to make out . “What?” He slowed and twisted to look at her. Her brows were low, lips pressed tightly closed . “Warlord’s cronies!” she spat. “The ones that have his mark .” A skull, fashioned in the shape of a gear. Understanding clicked into place. Now, he had another thing to call Warlord’s bots. Had anyone ever called them gearhead directly? They’d likely be just as confused as Ronin had been . “Yes. Most of the bots in Cheyenne don’t wear his symbol, but he always keeps the ones that do guarding the wall .” Lara frowned, jaw muscles ticking, and stared at the ground in front of her. She hooked her thumbs under the straps of her pack and adjusted its position on her shoulders . “What’s wrong?” he asked. Human expressions could be so telling, but Ronin wasn’t well-versed enough to distinguish their many nuances . “ Nothing .” Her posture belied her answer — the rigidity of her bent arms, her shuffling walk, her head tilted down . “Speak plainly, Lara Brooks. Are you concerned about Warlord’s gearheads ?” “Damn it,” she said, swinging her foot to kick a rock aside, “I don’t want to talk about it .” The rock clattered away, bouncing over the gravel before hitting an exposed railroad tie with a hollow thunk . Ronin halted. Startled, Lara nearly walked into him. He turned, and she looked up at him, squinting against the rain. Moisture clung to the lashes framing her bright blue eyes .” “Should I expect trouble?” he asked . “I don’t know .” “I need to know .” “And I don’t know. You’re a bot, you see his cronies in the Market all the time .” “I’ve never been to the Market with you. There a reason they’d look at you different than anyone else ?” “They don’t like humans.” She dropped her gaze . The rain filled the silence between them. Ronin’s processors would melt in trying to figure this woman out. Her reaction to him, when she’d first realized he was a bot, her adamant refusal to trade with him because of what he was, this aversion to Warlord’s enforcers … “What did they do to you , Lara ?” She was close enough that a slight move of his arm could settle his hand on her hip. How would she respond to such contact? Why did he want it? She’d suffered, somehow, and something hidden deep in his coding said to touch her, to hold her, until she felt better . “They didn’t do anything to me.” She lifted her chin and met his optics. “There’s nothing to talk about. We going, or what ?” Ronin curled his fingers into a fist and let it hang, useless, at his side. No touching; that was one of her conditions. Perhaps he’d been hasty in agreeing to it so easily. She was hiding something, but what could he do about it? Watch her dance, and provide for her. That was the deal . He resumed his walk, leading her through the gates into the Market. They’d used the large, heavy steel doors of a shipping container for the entrance, and Ronin had yet to see them closed . They moved past the Scrapper’s and half a dozen vendor stands, Lara no more than a single
